title: Extended buffer normalization
body:
_What are the reasons/motivation for this change?_

The previously merged support for buffer normalized RTLIL (bufnorm) was incomplete, in both design and implementation, in that it would only handle a subset of RTLIL. In particular it would break in unpredictable ways for unsupported input. This PR extends buffer normalization to cover a much larger RTLIL subset that should cover all RTLIL supported by any commonly used flow.

_Explain how this is achieved._

This PR extends the concept of buffer normalized RTLIL to

* Support inout ports, by always treating them as output ports as far as buffer normalization is concerned.
* Support undirected connections, represented using a new `$connect` cell that has two ports (modeled as input ports within RTLIL, but better thought of as inout ports).
* Supports representing undriven or ambiguously driven nets, represented as "driven" by `$buf`fered `'z` bits.
* Ensure every wire has a unique "driver"-cell by adding new `$input_port` cells as "driver" for input ports.

I'm using "driver" and "driven" in quotes for things that are considered the `driverCell` as far as bufnorm is concerned, even if they are not drivers in the EE sense.

This should ensure that for any RTLIL design there is a buffer normalized version of it that is connectivity-equivalent (i.e. equivalent when viewed via a `SigMap` or roughly equivalent in ways that `opt_clean` is allowed to change connections). The buffer normalized version can then be traversed without the need for a `SigMap` and all slicing and concatenation is done using `SigSpec`s on the input ports of cells, which also simplifies traversal and pattern matching.

Implementing this required a complete rewrite of the RTLIL-kernel code that performs buffer normalization. See the inline comments for how this new version works.

As part of this, deletion of Cells while the design is buffer normalized are now also supported. This works by deferring the actual `delete` of previously enqueued cells until the end of the next `bufNormalize` call.

Making sure roundtripping a design through bufnorm followed by `opt_clean` doesn't leave around any bufnorm-specific remnants also required small changes to some other passes (`opt_clean`, `simplemap` via `techmap` and `write_verilog`) to special case `$buf`fered `'z` bits. Since the `$buf` cell itself is a relatively recent addition and since this specific use of the `'z` bit does not match the previously supported pattern for tristate drivers, this shouldn't have any unintended effects on existing `'z` uses.

The experimental AIGER backend also needed some changes to support the RTLIL that resulted from the updated definition of buffer normalization. This consists primarily of changes to treat inout ports consistently as if they were output ports. The experimental AIGER backend also does not support `'z` bits in the way introduced by the new bufnorm code. Since I wanted to avoid making larger changes to the AIGER backend while simultaneously working out how to make bufnorm work for general RTLIL, I worked around this by introducing two helper passes that are now used by `abc_new`. They first hide and later recover the bufnorm introduced `'z` bits. With those in place, the AIGER backend will end up using blackboxes to represent both ambiguously driven nets and the undirected connections between them, ensuring no connectivity is lost while the design is processed by abc.

_If applicable, please suggest to reviewers how they can test the change._

This PR resolves https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/issues/5259 and, when using `bufnorm -update` instead of `bufnorm`, also https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/issues/5223.

Still missing is support for FF init values (was also previously missing from bufnorm, not relevant for ASIC flows), an updated `bufnorm` utility pass (only `bufnorm -update` and `bufnorm -reset` should be used for now), further test cases, and proper documentation of bufnorm.

It might make sense to address these missing parts in a follow up PR since this PR itself should be sufficient to unblock existing users of the experimental AIGER backend that are currently limited by the supported RTLIL subset and since the new bufnorm implementation passes all existing bufnorm tests (after adding `opt_clean` in a few places).

This PR also includes https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/pull/5351 which fixes an unrelated bug hit by the reproducer from https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys/issues/5259 after fixing the bufnorm issues.
